nodes.description: Menstrual disturbances including amenorrhea are part of the endocrine manifestations of the syndrome, potentially linked to hyperprolactinemia or hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna, acromegaly and hyperprolactinaemia.
evidence.snippet: Acromegalic patients with hyperprolactinaemia also expressed HFI in a higher proportion of individuals than the control group (P = 0.0001).

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive decline may develop, attributed to frontal lobe compression and atrophy from the expanding inner table of the skull.

evidence.reference_title: Evidence of a genetic basis of Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome. A case report of identical twins.
evidence.snippet: We report two 71-year-old female monozygotic twins presenting with advanced hyperostosis frontalis interna, obesity, shortness and cognitive impairment.

evidence.reference_title: [Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome. Case report and review of the literature].
evidence.snippet: A 74 years old female with a history of exposure to wood smoke, vitiligo, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension and cognitive impairment who enters the hospital by malaise, dizziness, anxiety, confusion, disorientation and difficulty walking.

nodes.description: Depressive symptoms are frequently reported and may be related to frontal lobe dysfunction or the broader neuropsychiatric component of the syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Neuropsychological profile of a male psychiatric patient with a Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome.
evidence.snippet: In 1928 Stewart and in 1930 Morel added neuropsychiatric symptoms, e.g. depression and dementia, which led to the definition of the Morgagni-Stewart-Morel Syndrome (MSM).

evidence.reference_title: Evidence of a genetic basis of Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome. A case report of identical twins.
evidence.snippet: Moreover, the patients showed some additional conditions only occurring in one individual or the other such as migraine, marked recurrent depressive disorder or polyarthrosis.

nodes.description: The precise pathogenesis of frontal bone thickening remains unclear. Well-supported hypotheses include prolonged estrogen exposure stimulating osteoblastic activity in the frontal bone inner table (supported by large-scale anthropological studies) and elevated leptin levels associated with obesity promoting bone formation. A more contested hypothesis involves dysregulated growth hormone secretion from pituitary microadenomatosis; however, recent evidence shows no direct causative role for GH excess or hyperprolactinemia in HFI etiopathogenesis. The condition predominantly affects females and is associated with age, being much less frequent in females under 40 years.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna: an anthropological perspective.
evidence.snippet: It is most commonly found among females and is believed to be associated with prolonged estrogen stimulation

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna: an anthropological perspective.
evidence.snippet: While its magnitude of manifestation and frequency are much higher in females, HFI is not a purely female phenomenon. Males with hormonal disturbances such as atrophic testis were found to manifest HFI type D.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis Frontalis Interna and a Question on Its Pathology: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: It is most often found in women after menopause. It is also associated with hormonal imbalance, being overweight, history of headaches, and neurocognitive degenerative conditions. Female gender, advanced age, extended estrogen stimulation, and elevated leptin levels may also play a role.

evidence.reference_title: Frequency of hyperostosis frontalis interna in patients with active acromegaly: is there a possible role of GH excess or hyperprolactinemia in its etiopathogenesis?
evidence.snippet: Although the frequency of HFI is 22% in patients with acromegaly, neither excess GH nor hyperprolactinemia plays a role in its etiopathogenesis. Various genetic or epigenetic factors may contribute to its etiology.

evidence.reference_title: Full penetrance of Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome in a 75-year-old woman: case report and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Endocrine or nutritional disorders may have led to an altered bone metabolism with frontal bone apposition.

nodes.description: Inappropriate lactation related to hyperprolactinemia, which has been found in a significant proportion of patients with hyperostosis frontalis interna.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna, acromegaly and hyperprolactinaemia.
evidence.snippet: The cause of HFI remains unknown but appears to be strongly associated with acromegaly, particularly in the presence of co-existent hyperprolactinaemia.

nodes.description: Chronic headaches are a common presenting symptom, potentially related to increased intracranial pressure from calvarial thickening or direct compression effects.

evidence.reference_title: Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome presenting with neurological symptoms: a case report.
evidence.snippet: We present the case of a 76-year-old patient who complained of confusion, extreme irritability, and headache and was diagnosed with MSM based on examination, imaging, and test results.

evidence.reference_title: Full penetrance of Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome in a 75-year-old woman: case report and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: A 75-year-old woman presented with severe frontal headache and a history of psychotic disorders.

nodes.description: Excess body hair growth in a male pattern distribution in affected females, related to the virilism component of the syndrome and potentially linked to hyperprolactinemia or androgen dysregulation.

evidence.reference_title: [Hyperostosis frontalis interna : etiology and differential diagnosis].
evidence.snippet: Hyperostosis frontalis interna was first described in 1719 in association with obesity and hirsutism, forming Morgagni's syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome presenting with neurological symptoms: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Morgagni-Stewart-Morel (MSM) syndrome is characterized by the thickening of the frontal bone of the skull (hyperostosis frontalis interna) obesity, neurological symptoms, and hypertrichosis.

nodes.description: Bilateral, irregular thickening of the inner table of the frontal bone, typically sparing the midline at the superior sagittal sinus. This is the defining radiological feature, usually discovered incidentally on skull X-ray, CT, or MRI.

evidence.reference_title: [Hyperostosis frontalis interna : etiology and differential diagnosis].
evidence.snippet: Hyperostosis frontalis interna was first described in 1719 in association with obesity and hirsutism, forming Morgagni's syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna: an anthropological perspective.
evidence.snippet: Hyperostosis frontalis interna (HFI) is manifested by the accretion of bone on the inner table of the frontal bone.

evidence.reference_title: [Hyperostosis frontalis interna : etiology and differential diagnosis].
evidence.snippet: The anomaly exclusively involves the inner table and constantly spares the diploe and the external table.

nodes.description: Hyperostosis frontalis interna is strongly associated with acromegaly and hyperprolactinemia, particularly when both conditions coexist. Elevated prolactin levels may contribute to menstrual disturbances, galactorrhea, and hirsutism observed in MSM syndrome. The relationship between HFI and hyperprolactinemia appears to be particularly strong in acromegalic patients.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna, acromegaly and hyperprolactinaemia.
evidence.snippet: Acromegalic patients with hyperprolactinaemia also expressed HFI in a higher proportion of individuals than the control group (P = 0.0001).

evidence.reference_title: Hyperostosis frontalis interna, acromegaly and hyperprolactinaemia.
evidence.snippet: The cause of HFI remains unknown but appears to be strongly associated with acromegaly, particularly in the presence of co-existent hyperprolactinaemia.

evidence.reference_title: Frequency of hyperostosis frontalis interna in patients with active acromegaly: is there a possible role of GH excess or hyperprolactinemia in its etiopathogenesis?
evidence.snippet: There was no difference between the HFI positive and negative acromegalic patients in basal GH, IGF-1, and PRL levels, IGF-1 index, diagnosis lag time, and insulin resistance.

nodes.description: Progressive frontal bone thickening can result in increased intracranial pressure due to reduced intracranial volume and potential obstruction of CSF flow pathways. This increased pressure may contribute to headaches and other neurological symptoms observed in MSM syndrome. The mechanism links the skeletal abnormality (hyperostosis) to neurological manifestations through biomechanical effects.

evidence.reference_title: [Morgagni-Stewart-Morel syndrome. Case report and review of the literature].
evidence.snippet: In she were performed imaging of the skull where was observed the presence of extensive hyperostosis frontalis interna, cortical atrophy and a left thalamic lacunar infarction.

nodes.description: Progressive thickening of the frontal bone inner table can lead to compression of underlying brain parenchyma, particularly the frontal lobes. This mechanical compression may result in cerebral atrophy, cognitive impairment, neuropsychiatric symptoms including depression and anxiety, headaches, seizures, and cranial nerve dysfunction affecting olfaction and vision.

evidence.snippet: the severity of our patient's neurological and psychiatric symptoms correlates well with the severity of her hyperostosis frontalis interna and the cortical atrophy.

nodes.description: Central obesity is a cardinal feature, present in the majority of affected individuals. It may be related to endocrine dysregulation including leptin resistance and growth hormone abnormalities.

evidence.snippet: During this hospital stay the presence of grade I obesity, hyperglycemia, hypertriglyceridemia and hyperuricemia was documented.

evidence.snippet: Morgagni-Stewart-Morel (MSM) syndrome is characterized by the thickening of the frontal bone of the skull (hyperostosis frontalis interna) obesity, neurological symptoms, and hypertrichosis.

nodes.description: Generalized seizures may occur, potentially related to frontal lobe compression from the hyperostotic bone. Seizures have been reported since early adulthood in some patients.

evidence.snippet: They both have suffered from generalized seizures since their early adulthood.

nodes.description: Dizziness and vertigo are reported neurological symptoms, potentially related to cranial nerve involvement or intracranial pressure changes.
